skill.name: pdf
skill.description: PDF processing toolkit for extracting text and tables, creating and modifying PDFs, splitting and merging files, rendering pages, and filling PDF forms.
skill.summary: Process, analyze, create, and fill PDF documents with Python libraries and command-line tools.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no evidence of malware, credential theft, prompt injection, or hidden network exfiltration. Most hits are PDF command examples, local file reads and writes, PDF field names, or documentation URLs. The skill still warrants a medium risk label because it ships scripts that process untrusted PDFs and write user-selected output files.

medium_findings.title: Local PDF Scripts Read and Write User Files
medium_findings.description: The included scripts parse PDFs, read JSON field data, render images, and write PDFs, images, or JSON outputs to caller-provided paths. This is legitimate for a PDF toolkit, but untrusted PDFs and broad output paths can expose users to parser bugs, accidental overwrites, or sensitive document handling mistakes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads and writes are direct and intentional. I found no exfiltration or destructive behavior, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: External PDF Commands Require Controlled Inputs
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to run PDF command-line tools such as pdftotext, qpdf, pdftk, pdfimages, and pdftoppm. These are normal PDF workflows, but commands should be run only on trusted paths and within an expected working directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible examples and not hidden execution. Risk remains because users may run them against arbitrary PDF files or shell paths.

medium_findings.title: Referenced Schematic Helper Is Not Present in Scanned Files
medium_findings.description: The main instructions recommend running a schematic generation script for visual PDF content. The reviewed file set does not include that helper, so users could be confused or route work to an unavailable external skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reference is explicit in the skill instructions, but I found no evidence of a malicious implementation in the scanned files. This is a supply-chain and transparency concern, not a confirmed exploit.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Hits Are PDF Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography indicators align with PDF encryption examples, coordinates, field values, or ordinary text in documentation and tests. I found no custom cryptographic implementation or insecure hash use in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding lines show PDF password or field examples rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found for malicious or obsolete crypto use.

low_findings.title: Static C2 Keyword Hits Are Benign PDF Examples
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword matches occur in normal PDF sample code and field handling context, not in command-and-control networking logic. No evidence found for beacons, callbacks, encoded endpoints, or remote tasking.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited regions are merge and page-manipulation examples. They do not contain network clients or remote control behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to license terms, PDF specifications, or a library issue used as explanatory references. I found no code that sends document contents or credentials to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in legal text or comments only. No HTTP client, fetch call, or upload path was found at these locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Output Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: Several scripts accept output paths from command-line arguments and write files there. This is expected for document processing, but callers should avoid privileged directories and confirm overwrite behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path arguments and writes are clear. I did not find path traversal logic or privileged file targeting built into the scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: PDF Parser Exposure to Untrusted Documents
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts pass user PDFs into pypdf, pdf2image, and image libraries. This is core functionality, but malformed PDFs can trigger parser vulnerabilities in dependencies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dependency exposure is inherent and observable. I found no suspicious wrapper behavior beyond local parsing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Recipes in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes many shell recipes for PDF processing. The examples are not automatically executed, but users should quote paths and review commands before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit documentation, not hidden code execution. Risk depends on how users adapt them with real file names.

content.user_title: Process PDFs with Extraction and Form Tools
content.value_statement: PDF workflows often require many libraries and careful form handling. This skill provides practical recipes and scripts for extraction, conversion, creation, merging, splitting, and form filling.
actual_capabilities: Extract text, metadata, tables, and page-level content from PDFs using Python libraries.
Create, merge, split, rotate, watermark, encrypt, and crop PDF files with documented recipes.
Render PDF pages to images for OCR, visual review, and form-field placement.
Detect fillable PDF fields and generate field metadata for form completion.
Fill fillable forms or add text annotations to non-fillable forms using helper scripts.
Validate form bounding boxes before writing annotations to a PDF.
limitations: It depends on local Python packages and external PDF utilities that may not be installed.
Scanned PDFs require OCR tools and may produce imperfect text extraction results.
Complex PDF forms can render differently across viewers after values are written.
The skill does not guarantee safe handling of malicious or corrupted PDFs.

use_cases.title: Extract Research Tables
use_cases.description: Pull text and tables from reports or papers for review, cleanup, and spreadsheet export.

use_cases.title: Prepare Business Documents
use_cases.description: Merge, split, rotate, watermark, or password-protect PDFs during document operations.

use_cases.title: Complete PDF Forms
use_cases.description: Inspect form fields, validate bounding boxes, and produce filled PDFs from structured user data.

prompt_templates.title: Extract PDF Text
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for plain text extraction.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the readable text from this PDF. Preserve page order and note pages where text extraction fails.

prompt_templates.title: Merge and Organize PDFs
prompt_templates.prompt: Merge these PDFs in the order I provide. Remove blank pages, rotate pages that are sideways, and create a clean final PDF.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Tables in a Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Find all tables in this PDF report. Summarize each table and export the extracted rows to a spreadsheet-friendly format.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a Non-Fillable Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Fill this non-fillable PDF form using the provided user details. Convert pages to images, identify entry boxes, validate bounding boxes, and create the completed PDF.

output_examples.input: A 12-page quarterly report with several embedded financial tables.
output_examples.output: A page-by-page text summary, a list of extracted tables, and a spreadsheet-ready table export.

output_examples.input: Three separate PDFs that need one combined client packet.
output_examples.output: A merged PDF with corrected page orientation, removed blank pages, and retained source order.

output_examples.input: A scanned application form and user-provided field values.
output_examples.output: A completed PDF with text placed in the detected entry areas and validation notes for reviewed boxes.

best_practices: Run PDF scripts in a dedicated working directory with copies of source documents.
Review extracted text, tables, and filled forms visually before sharing results.
Keep PDF libraries and command-line utilities updated before processing untrusted files.
anti_patterns: Do not run shell commands on file names that you have not reviewed or quoted safely.
Do not process sensitive PDFs in shared directories without access controls.
Do not assume OCR or table extraction is correct without manual validation.

faq.question: Can this skill fill PDF forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports fillable forms and non-fillable forms that need text annotations.

faq.question: Does it work with scanned PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes, but scanned PDFs need OCR tools. OCR accuracy depends on scan quality.

faq.question: Can it extract tables?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes pdfplumber examples for table extraction and spreadsheet-friendly processing.

faq.question: Does it need external programs?
faq.answer: Some workflows use tools such as qpdf, pdftotext, pdfimages, pdftoppm, or OCR utilities.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential PDFs?
faq.answer: Use a private working directory and verify local processing settings before handling confidential documents.

faq.question: Can it repair damaged PDFs?
faq.answer: It documents qpdf repair commands, but severe corruption may require specialized PDF repair tools.
